input_row.title: Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens
input_row.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
input_row.rationale: Stored access policies provide centralized control over SAS token access, allowing administrators to update permissions or revoke access. This approach strengthens security by reducing the risk of unauthorized access to storage resources. Impact: There is no cost for creating stored access policies, however there is some administrative overhead involved in managing these policies.
input_row.remediation: Default Value: By default, stored access policies are not associated with SAS. To use a stored access policy, it must be explicitly created and linked to the SAS at the time of creation. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas- overview#best-practices-when-using-sas 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/define-stored-access- policy Additional Information: This recommendation forms the basis for the recommendations with the same title in the following sections: • Azure Blob Storage • Queue Storage • Storage Explorer
input_row.impact: There is no cost for creating stored access policies, however there is some administrative overhead involved in managing these policies.
input_row.reference: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas- overview#best-practices-when-using-sas 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/define-stored-access- policy Additional Information: This recommendation forms the basis for the recommendations with the same title in the following sections: • Azure Blob Storage • Queue Storage • Storage Explorer

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access the Azure Portal:**
   - Log into your Azure account and navigate to the Azure Portal.

2. **Locate the Storage Resource:**
   - In the left sidebar, select "Storage accounts," and then click on the specific storage account you wish to inspect.

3. **Review Shared Access Signatures (SAS) Policies:**
   - Navigate to the "Access control (IAM)" section or "Shared access signature" section based on the specific storage type.
   - Look for any existing stored access policies associated with SAS tokens.

4. **Verify Policy Association:**
   - Ensure that the SAS tokens in use are associated with stored access policies. Confirm that they are not using ad-hoc SAS tokens that lack the centralized management benefits of stored access policies.

5. **Check for Policy Management:**
   - If stored access policies exist, check the details for permissions, expiry, and revocation settings.
   - Ensure that all SAS tokens are governed by these policies to confirm compliance.

6. **Expected Secure Configuration:**
   - Confirm that all SAS tokens are linked to stored access policies, which provide centralized control over permissions, expiration, and revocation.

7. **How to Confirm Compliance:**
   - Document any findings, noting whether SAS tokens are being generated with stored access policies. If not, note the instances where ad-hoc SAS tokens are identified.
  
This manual verification will allow you to ensure compliance with the control regarding the use of stored access policies for SAS tokens in Azure storage services.
